original_abstract: Abstract Neuroscience has long been an essential driver of progress in artificial intelligence (AI). We propose that to accelerate progress in AI, we must invest in fundamental research in NeuroAI. A core component of this is the embodied Turing test, which challenges AI animal models to interact with the sensorimotor world at skill levels akin to their living counterparts. The embodied Turing test shifts the focus from those capabilities like game playing and language that are especially well-developed or uniquely human to those capabilities – inherited from over 500 million years of evolution – that are shared with all animals. Building models that can pass the embodied Turing test will provide a roadmap for the next generation of AI.
humanized_abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) research has long been fueled by advances in neuroscience. Now, scientists argue that the field needs more “basic science” on neuro-computing, or NeuroAI research. They say that for real AI advancements to occur, embodied Turing test “animal-like” robots that can navigate and interact with the environment at the same level of proficiency as real animals need to be created. The embodied Turing test moves away from focusing on skills to which humans and computers are naturally predisposed such as playing games or speaking human language and instead towards other abilities that are evolutionary shared among all types of animals for over half a billion years. Passing the test would give AI researchers an idea of the characteristics of future AI systems.

Passing the test would give AI researchers an idea of the characteristics of future AI systems.
